text_blocks.text: Inflammation is one of the
first responses of the immune system to
infection. The symptoms of inflammation
are redness, swelling, heat, and pain,
which are caused by increased blood
flow into tissue. Inflammation is produced
by eicosanoids and cytokines, which are
released by injured or infected cells.
Eicosanoids include prostaglandins that
produce fever and the dilation of blood
vessels associated with inflammation, and
leukotrienes that attract certain white
blood cells (leukocytes). Common cytokines
include interleukins that are responsible
for communication between white blood
cells; chemokines that promote chemotaxis;
and interferons that have anti-viral
effects, such as shutting down protein
synthesis in the host cell. Growth factors
and cytotoxic factors may also be released.
These cytokines and other chemicals
recruit immune cells to the site of
infection and promote healing of any
damaged tissue following the removal of
pathogens.
